design_philosophy.core_loop: Advance through linear level, defeat waves of enemy ninjas using basic attacks and power-ups, defeat stage boss to progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Time pressure combined with enemy density; players must defeat objectives before time expires while managing health via scattered power-ups
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as tough street brawlers rescuing the President from ninja criminals; 1980s action movie hero power fantasy with comedic tone
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately absurdist premise (ninja kidnapping president) paired with earnest beat-em-up execution; embraces '80s action clichés; cooperative play emphasizes two-player heroic narrative; stage variety (city, truck, sewer, forest, train, cave, factory) prevents mechanical monotony

difficulty.lives_system: Player health bar with scattered health power-ups throughout levels; multiple hits required to defeat player character
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; each level ends with boss encounter; no mid-level checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Power-up collection (weapons like knives/nunchakus, health restoration, time extensions) allows skilled players to manage difficulty; charging mechanic for enhanced attacks provides risk/reward timing decisions

progression.upgrade_system: Temporary weapon pick-ups (knives, nunchakus) found during levels; charging punch ability available from start but improves through mastery
progression.unlock_mechanism: Boss defeats gate progression to next stage; seven distinct stages with escalating enemy variety and spatial complexity
replayability_hooks: Two-player cooperative gameplay creates different skill combinations and emergent strategies
Arcade version features multiple epilogues (US vs Japan) providing different narrative closure
Weapon availability placement encourages route optimization and speedrun potential
Boss patterns favor pattern recognition allowing skilled players to achieve no-hit runs

world_building_techniques: Opening cutscene establishes premise with humorous formality ('Rampant ninja related crimes these days... Whitehouse is not the exception')
Environmental progression through recognizable locations (NYC streets, truck, sewer, forest, train, cave, factory) creates implicit narrative arc of pursuit
Boss character design (Karnov cameo, Kamui with illusions, Animal as behemoth, weapon-specialized warriors) suggests organized enemy hierarchy
Post-level victory poses and '"I'm bad!"' proclamations reinforce comedic hero fantasy
Epilogue celebration (hamburgers with President) provides narrative closure with tonal consistency

boss_design.philosophy: Each boss represents a distinct ninja warrior archetype or weapon tradition; bosses gate level progression and provide skill tests distinct from standard enemy patterns; cameo appearances (Karnov) serve as world-building and franchise references
notable_bosses: Karnov - Fire-breathing warrior; cameo from Data East's Karnov; introduces boss-specific special attacks
Iron Arm - Talon-wielding ninja; specialized weapon-based moveset
Kamui - Ninja master with illusion copying ability; introduces pattern complexity through duplication
Animal - Behemoth character; non-ninja archetype adding variety to boss roster
Devil Pole - Bōjutsu (staff) master; final pre-final boss
Dragon Ninja - Leader of criminal organization; final boss fight on helicopter; special themed background music (similar to Karnov main theme)

faction_indicators: Dragon Ninja gang - organized criminal ninja organization kidnapping political leaders
Bad Dudes - unaffiliated street brawlers positioned as heroic counter-force
Secret Service - government authority requesting civilian intervention
Boss hierarchy - specialized warriors (Karnov, Kamui, etc.) suggest structured criminal organization
danger_signals: Rampant ninja-related crimes affecting government security
Presidential kidnapping suggesting high-level criminal capability
Escalating enemy types (basic ninjas → kunoichi → fire-users → animal companions) indicating widening criminal network
Boss warrior specializations suggesting trained, hierarchical organization
environmental_storytelling: Urban progression (NYC streets → truck → underground sewer) implies criminal network embedded in city infrastructure
Train stage with Chelnov frozen cargo suggests criminal diversification beyond ninja operations
Underground factory headquarters establishes hidden power base and manufacturing capability
Spatial progression from public spaces (streets) to hidden locations (cave, factory) reflects criminal concealment strategy
